responseSet.title: Mourning Before the Fact
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning Before the Fact

Albert Rosner M.D.

Albert Rosner

Albert Rosner  

SUMMARY

The anticipation of the death of a substitute object and the accompanying mourning are defensive operations against the fear of loss of a more significant object. This variety of defense against loss may participate in the resolution of the oedipus complex during the phallic phase. This was demonstrated in a patient who during analysis had a premonitory dream of his father's death, which represented a defense against the fear of losing his mother.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Remarks on Anxiety Dreams in Latency and Adolescence

Nathan Root M.D.

Nathan Root

Nathan Root  

PSYCHOANALYTIC work with children soon demonstrates the truth of Anna Freud's (3) remark that their dreams are not so simple as one might think from the examples given in The Interpretation of Dreams(4). One may find much distortion of the latent dream thoughts in the dreams of young child patients, but even more in those of children from mid-latency on, and especially in anxiety dreams. The degree of dream distortion can often be related to the degree of anxiety or severity of conflictual disturbance and to the degree of resistance. It may very well be that our current delving more into the analytic study of more severe character problems, as with adult cases, brings us more instances wherein dreaming is more distorted and the youngsters have greater difficulty in dealing analytically with their dreams. Dream distortion presents one difficulty to the understanding of dreams. Beyond indicating that greater anxiety will increase the need for a stronger defense, which in turn can stimulate the censoring forces with resultant greater distortion when the dream is created, I can add nothing to Freud's findings about the various factors entering into dream distortion. It might be rewarding to study such factors comparatively among those analysands who work well with their dreams.

The inability to use the free-association method with children is another difficulty in analyzing their dreams. On the basis of this lack, some have denigrated all of child analysis.

Read at the Panel on The Role of the Dream in Child Analysis, Fall Meeting, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 4, 1959.

Submitted March 10, 1960

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Problems in Contemporary Ego Psychology

Bernard Apfelbaum Ph.D.

Bernard Apfelbaum

Bernard Apfelbaum  

THE ESSENTIAL feat which contemporary ego theorists claim to have accomplished is to have provided the ego with additional motive power without effecting a corresponding diminution in the role of the instinctual drives (6). The popularity of this theoretical innovation rests on the belief that it takes account of increased clinical emphasis on ego functioning while leaving fundamental assumptions regarding instinctual drives intact. This paper presents the proposition that contemporary ego psychology has not accomplished this end.

The Ego and Id in Freud's Work

In the context of Freud's early, provisional theoretical model, the ego was an inherently potent force in psychic functioning. It was conceived as an autonomous structure, operating with power provided by its own energy source (the self-preservation instinct) (2), (13). Freud's later emphasis on aggression eclipsed the role of the ego instincts (3), (8). The ego became a system established through experience, with no primary motive power of its own—the rider who must hold the superior strength of the horse (id) in check, often obliged to guide it where it wants to go (and unable to dismount) (3). The ego also had to contend with the superego, a system originally developed by the ego as an aid to repression which exacts its cost through lifelong domination of the ego.

[[RunningHead]]

Future Plans of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education

Emanuel Windholz M.D.

Emanuel Windholz

Emanuel Windholz  

In these few minutes which we have left at the conclusion of today's Plenary Session I will confine my remarks to three topics only. The papers presented this morning have already indicated the various directions of development. I can also refer you to my reports submitted to the Board on Professional Standards, particularly those on December 7, 1960 and May 3, 1961.

The reports on the Curriculum Conferences which were presented to you this morning, as well as other pertinent information which has been accumulated by the participants, will be available to a new Committee on Curriculum. Its composition will be announced as soon as it is constituted.1 This Committee will formulate its own methods, select appropriate topics, and consider the participation of Institutes. COFE will receive their suggestions and coordinate their plans with other plans.

One among these which is closely related to the curriculum is postgraduate education. It was first discussed as a plan for a postgraduate course to be offered to promising young graduates interested in teaching. The value of such course, however, depends on many factors, some of which can be assessed by a study of past experiences. A discussion of an extensive analysis of the Ernst Kris Study Group submitted to us by Charles Brenner and a similar report by Jacob Arlow opened up a number of problems: selection of participants, the role of the leader, of group formation, etc. A study of the postgraduate development of our young members appears as a necessity and a logical basis for our future planning.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of the Critic and Psychological Criticism

Philip Weissman M.D.

Philip Weissman

Philip Weissman  

Psychology of the Critic

WHATEVER ELSE a critic may or should be, he is minimally required to be a connoisseur. In any field of human activity, some are designated as critic or connoisseur by society. In preliterate society, the leader or the master was the connoisseur. The step from connoisseur to critic implies the progression from knowledge to judgment. Connoisseurship is related to scholarship, and criticism superimposes the factor of judgmental values. Many fields of man's endeavor call upon the critical connoisseur to play the role of historian, evaluator, judge, arbitrator, attributor, and interpreter.

When confined to the fields of artistic creativity, the critic must have a sound knowledge of man's art treasure, past and present. He must have the ability to identify the artist and his works, the skill and the knowledge to attribute unidentified treasures of art to the proper era and the proper artist. Ideally speaking, he should have insight into the nature of the artist and the creative process, and how they are integrated. For these functions, it is apparent that psychoanalytic intuition or orientation would be desirable. A full discussion of this matter is reserved for the second part of this study.

It is not uncommon for a critic to be devoid of creative powers in the given field of art he is serving. Critics are rarely creative as painters, and creative painters are rarely important as critics.

Submitted February 20, 1961

745

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

American Psychoanalytic Education—Historical Comments

Bertram Lewin M.D.

Bertram Lewin

Bertram Lewin  

The publication of the book, Psychoanalytic Education in the United States, has had several interesting side results. A valuable one has been to arouse the attention and thought of experienced and knowledgeable outside educators in what we are doing, and some of them have expressed their views on education and on us in writing. Among these critics, Seward Hiltner, Professor of Theology at the University of Chicago, has written an article I have been privileged to read in manuscript, which contains this flattering comment on the service to education by psychoanalysts.

The service is plainly magnificent and munificent. … Even if the motives for this service are as mixed as psychoanalysis demonstrates all human motivation to be, the conclusion is plain that of him who has more, more is expected, in other words that he exercises orders of professional responsibility which are vital to the existence of the profession at all, not even considered by the average practitioner. Psychoanalytic reluctance over claiming moral virtue ought certainly not to obscure the facts. And perhaps it would not be out of accord with psychoanalytic wisdom to suggest that inverted pride is still pride.

When we recall how often psychoanalysts have been asked to consider their evil ways and repent, it is certainly refreshing and gratifying to find a theologian, who asks us to stop and consider whether we are not repressing an unconscious sense of virtue. If Dr. Hiltner is correct, we must admit that, for all our insistent and constant need for insight, we have rarely or ever thought of the matter and have taken our service in this field for granted. The duty to educate must have been built into our archaic psychoanalytic ego very early in our history, and it would indeed be proper for us to dig it up. It seems that every psychoanalyst has in his knapsack a schoolmaster's ferule. We find him teaching not only in our own institutes but in schools of social work, in psychiatric and other university departments, in hospitals and agencies, in extension courses of all sorts. In many capacities, official and unofficial, continuous and sporadic, the psychoanalyst is in fact a teacher. This fact also did not escape Dr. Hiltner's notice and gracious comment.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation

Irwin Marcus M.D.

Irwin Marcus

Irwin Marcus  

In her opening remarks, Bibring noted that masturbation is a borderline phenomenon which is accepted as both a normal biopsychological process and one that is often treated as pathological. She struck the keynote of the panel in suggesting the continued need for our scientific inquiries into this ubiquitous phenomenon, to further our understanding of the place and function of masturbation.

Irwin M. Marcus presented a historical survey of the literature. The many and varied contributions he briefly summarized included references to Freud, the 1912 Discussion, Annie Reich, Anna Freud, Ernst Kris, Bornstein and others.

In order to devote this report to the stimulating clinical considerations of the panel, I will not dwell on the valuable contributions of the past. Suffice it to say that masturbation, masturbation conflicts, and the associated fantasies and dreams were recognized early by Freud. Freud observed the occurrence of symptoms and clinical syndromes as related to conflicts in this area and later broadened the concept of masturbation to include all forms of autoerotic stimulation. As Sidney Levin pointed out in the discussion, Freud emphasized that pathological fixations could arise either from excessive suppression or inadequate suppression of masturbation and masturbation fantasies.

Presented at the Fiftieth Anniversary Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, May, 1961. Chairman: Grete L. Bibring, M.D.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disturbance of Ego Functions of Speech and Abstract Thinking

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

SUMMARY

I have suggested that disconnected speech and disturbances in abstract thinking serve many defensive needs and may have their roots in the fantasy of the mother-child single body image. The illogical speech does not permit communication, social relations, and the expression of the affective states of love and hate. The fusion fantasy facilitates a significant impairment of the ego in the operation and development of the function of secondary-process thinking by blocking the introjection of the image of mother's body. With the need to deny separation from mother, it is not possible for the child to see her, to have her become an object for identification. It also permits denial of the reality of the femaleness of mother. The psychotic or borderline mother who blandly exhibits her nakedness to her son throughout his life to maturity undoubtedly imposes upon him the need to deny what he had seen and reinforces the operation of an archaic mechanism of denial. This in turn fosters the fantasy of fusion with her. The failure to incorporate, to introject, and to identify with the mother damages the ego functions of speech and thought processes as they involve abstract thinking.

[[RunningHead]]

Ego Functions and Bodily Reactions

Samuel Silverman M.D.

Samuel Silverman

Samuel Silverman  

EARLY CLASSICAL psychoanalytic references to bodily reactions associated with psychic disturbances emphasized the role of libido in cathecting body parts and were made in connection with such conditions as conversion hysteria and hypochondriasis. Later when Freud (6) introduced the structural concept of the mental apparatus, he pointed out that the body, particularly its surface, was the source of sensations from which the ego is derived. Schilder (20) elaborated on this by postulating the existence of a body image; i.e., the body and its organs are represented intrapsychically by means of a sum of memories of body sensations and their interrelations. The body image was shown to be not necessarily identical with the objective body. These two concepts became additional influences in the continuing study of the relationship of bodily reactions to psychic functions as this study was extended to the so-called psychosomatic disorders and organ neuroses. While during this time the related subject of ego functioning also became a focus of attention, it was not until recently that its connection with bodily processes began to be investigated.

In 1939 Felix Deutsch (1) pointed out that the ego utilizes sense perceptions to prevent or deny a loss by their symbolic representation of the object. Subsequently Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein (11) suggested that since the ego as a functioning organization controls motility, there is a particularly close tie between ego, aggression, and the skeletal musculature. [[RunningHead]]

Scientific Proceedings—panel Reports—Classical Forms of Neurosis in Infancy and Early Childhood

Elizabeth Kaplan M.D.

Elizabeth Kaplan

Elizabeth Kaplan  

This panel supplements the discussion on Problems of Infantile Neurosis presented at the New York Psychoanalytic Society, May, 1954. Lucie Jessner introduced the panel by referring to case studies in the literature of carefully analyzed neurotic children, and to the Arden House Symposium, 1954, which Ernst Kris chaired. Jessner said that the topic "infantile neurosis" allows for several lines of approach: first from the reconstruction of the past in analyzed adults, and second from the study of children's acute neuroses. She mentioned the two groups of neurotic manifestations in early childhood: (1) transient hysterical, phobic, compulsive reactions seen in the course of normal child development, subsiding when a further step in maturation is reached; (2) less transitory disturbances that lead to an impairment of ego development, totally or partially, by restricting, distorting, or crippling certain ego functions; the conflict is at least partially internalized. As analysts, we are interested in the genesis of a disturbance, and look backward as well as forward. She thought this panel promised to integrate the wealth of knowledge accumulated in the last decade through careful observation of children and through longitudinal studies.

Julius Richmond presented a paper on "Some Direct Observations of Disordered Behavior in Infants." From his experience as a pediatrician, he studied the complexity of the parent-infant interaction and their influence on the direction of development. His paper was based on observation of clinical material, mostly of infants from six months to one year of age.

Presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1961. Chairman: Lucie Jessner, M.D.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Development and the Prevention of Mental Illness

Manuel Furer M.D.

Manuel Furer

Manuel Furer  

Herbert Waldhorn pointed out that in the recent past psychoanalysis had mainly been concerned with the inevitability of neurotic conflict and the great complexity of overdetermination in mental functioning; consequently, the problem of the prevention of mental illness has seemed "awesomely unapproachable." However, with the recent advances in our knowledge of early psychic functioning deriving from both clinical psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic research in childhood, the time has come to attempt to rethink the problem.

To illustrate this change in point of view, he quoted Anna Freud from her Arden House talk in 1954, in which she stated that the emergence of neurotic conflicts had to be regarded as the price paid for the complexity of the human personality. In 1957, however, in her paper on "Child Observation and the Prediction of Development, " she paid tribute to the late Ernst Kris's ideas about prediction and the early spotting of danger, adding that prevention was feasible only if we could know the therapeutic steps which are appropriate to each age level and its disturbances, and to each special group of disorders. The new knowledge, which Kris had stressed and which now made such an approach to prevention possible, consists in the awareness of the mother's unique role in human life, the adaptive value of psychic conflict and function, the interaction of libidinal and aggressive drives, and the phase-specific and situation-specific mechanisms of defense.

In September, 1960, Anna Freud gave her revisions of her contributions to child analysis. She proposed a new method of diagnosis in childhood, which would be concerned with the assessment of lines of development, rather than with symptom formation, levels of achievement in the ego, or instinctual phase. She discussed the importance of progressive and regressive trends and again raised the possibility of developing specifically appropriate types of treatment and preventive intervention.

Marc Hollender

Marc Hollender  

IN A LETTER written on June 9, 1899 (1, Letter 108), Freud stated: "There is one wish that every dream is intended to fulfill, though it assumes various forms. That is the wish to sleep. You dream to avoid having to wake up, because you want to sleep." In The Interpretation of Dreams, Freud repeatedly reasserted that the wish to sleep is "one of the motives for the formation of dreams" (2, p. 234). The emphatic quality of his viewpoint was evident when he referred to the wish to sleep as "this universal, invariably present and unchanging wish to sleep …" (2, p. 234). And, " Dreams are the GUARDIANS of sleep and not its disturbers " (2, p. 233).

This viewpoint has been so generally accepted that it might be assumed that it has been validated by time and experience. I would like to suggest, however, that while it may be true that dreams often (or even, most often) do preserve sleep, this is not invariably the motive for dreaming. In other words, we may be confusing a consequence with a motive.

In re-examining this subject, I shall discuss the basis for refuting Freud's thesis that the wish to sleep is a universal motive for dreaming and the reason why this generalization was strongly defended by him.

I

In children (and less strikingly in adults) there may be a conflict between two biological needs.

Professor and Chairman, Department of Psychiatry, State University of New York, Upstate Medical Center, and Director, Syracuse Psychiatric Hospital.

Submitted December 27, 1960

1Although the distinction between a need and a wish should be made, it is not crucial to the issue under consideration. It is not my purpose to discuss the general wish-fulfillment thesis at this time. In so far as possible, I will use Freud's basic theory concerning dreams, since it is my intention to focus only on the question: is the wish to sleep a universal motive for dreaming?323

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blushing, Fear of Blushing, and Shame

Sandor Feldman M.D.

Sandor Feldman

Sandor Feldman  

SUMMARY

In my first paper (1922) I considered shame and blushing the result of repression of sexual excitation. The repression was due to castration threat. The repressed sexual (genital and pregenital) excitation was displaced—as in conversion hysteria—from below upward, from the genitals to the face. I thought at that time that females experience shame more than do males.

In my second paper (1941) I looked at shame and blushing from the ego's relation to and blind dependence on the superego. The superego was an external threat, instead of being an internalized, silent, directing and independent agency.

In the present paper I state my views that the desire to be the focus of attention is the key drive in blushers. This drive is universal; in blushers it is exceedingly strong. Males blush just as females do. Imposed inhibition of heterosexual drives is responded to in male blushers by assuming a feminine role, which also has to be repressed; the demand to be ashamed is then experienced. In female blushers it might result in desiring to have a male organ on the female body, and not to give up femininity. Females are ashamed when they want attention as females, and males, also for the same drive, to get attention as females.

Cognizance is taken in this paper of an agency in the ego which sides with the id drives. In therapy this agency has to be clearly isolated in order that the patient should be able to establish a working cooperation between the two sides of the ego, one which is turned toward the id, and the other which is in contact with external reality.

Arthur Brenner

THE THIRTY-EIGHTH chapter of Genesis preserves for all time the tragic and shameful memory of Onan: he spilled his seed on the ground and the Lord slew him. He thus gave his name to a synonym for masturbation and he still furnishes the key text for the religiously sanctioned condemnation of contraception. Onan, however, is only a minor character, and his story is merely an incidental episode in the over-all drama of Genesis xxxviii—a Bible story which points a totally different moral.

And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar. And Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the Lord; and the Lord slew him. [[RunningHead]]

Development of the Educational Program

Sara Bonnett M.D.

Sara Bonnett

Sara Bonnett  

Almost exactly two years ago, in May, 1959, the American Psychoanalytic Association undertook to sponsor a program of long-range studies on psychoanalytic education. This decision was the outgrowth of a persistent need, of which the first organized expression was a plan for a Survey of Psychoanalytic Education. The necessity of a preliminary Survey had been recognized as early as 1947. After lying fallow for some years, this idea gained progressively in strength and conviction, until, in December, 1954, a Survey Committee was given the responsibility of developing plans for a Survey Project and seeking funds for its support.

More detailed plans were carried forward by a Survey Steering Committee under the able chairmanship of Dr. Joan Fleming. It organized the Preliminary Commissions, on which all our institutes were represented, for consideration of the various broad divisions of psychoanalytic education requiring study and investigation. They issued a series of papers, known as the Rainbow Report, which was submitted to all educational committees of institutes for comment. The year 1956 finally saw the materialization of a project for a Survey of Psychoanalytic Education, with Dr. Bertram Lewin and Miss Helen Ross as co-directors.

The end of the Survey Field Study was signalized by the writing of the book, Psychoanalytic Education in the U.S., under the joint authorship of the Survey directors. Thanks to their untiring efforts and their sensitive approach to the institutes, we had at our command a wealth of meticulously documented facts never before available. Many of these appear in the book, many more were arranged skillfully into important and almost encyclopedic files, which have been kept up to date.

As the Survey project drew to a close, the Association was confronted with what the next step should be. The Survey had never been considered a goal in itself, not an end point. It was essential that a bridge be created, one which could lead us from the known facts into an exploration of the problems They revealed. [[RunningHead]]

Editor's Note

The Annual Meetings in 1961 were dedicated to the Fiftieth Anniversary of the founding of the American Psychoanalytic Association. It seemed singularly fitting to celebrate on this occasion the pre-eminent position of the Association as the representative of the science of psychoanalysis in America by directing attention to the steadily increasing and important role it has assumed in fostering psychoanalytic education. Accordingly, a section of the meetings was devoted to a plenary session at which reports were heard on the development of the educational program, the consultation service, the conference on curriculum, and the future plans of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education. The enthusiasm and the warm interest displayed in these proceedings by the membership prompted the Executive Committee to recommend to the Editorial Board the publication of these reports. A fitting background to the reports is a historical survey of American Psychoanalytic Education in a paper which was also presented at these meetings.

The Consultation Service

Bertram Lewin M.D.

Bertram Lewin

Bertram Lewin  

This morning's main events are the reports you will hear from Drs. Astley and Kohut concerning the studies undertaken by the Conferences on Curriculum, and I should like to take advantage of this occasion to congratulate them and their colleagues in the three Conferences on their progress in formulating and mutually discussing and clarifying the problems in that particular field of psychoanalytic education. This presentation of the work of the Consultation Service will furnish the background for these coming reports. I shall not speak of the expanding work of the whole educational program but of the part with which I am most familiar, namely, the work and program of the consultants and of our service.

When Helen Ross and I were hired in 1959 as consultants, the general idea was that knowledge and experience acquired through three years' work on the Survey might somehow he put to work directly and concretely to advance psychoanalytic education. We had accumulated shelves of loose-leaf ring books, boxes, and filing cases bulging with documents of all sorts: records of interviews with faculty members, students, graduates, administrative staff; records of audits of classes and supervisory hours; extensive notes of observation in all fields of psychoanalytic education. We had correspondence and notes of consultations with experts outside of the narrower field. We had gathered syllabi, brochures, reports of institutes and committees, all sorts of records from the institute offices, and answers to the several questionnaires we had sent out to faculty and students. There was instructive material in these records, not all of it utilized for our reports, that suggested the possibility of further use. One of our knowledgeable colleagues, visiting us, said that we had enough to keep a whole department of graduate students busy. But a research factory was not precisely what we and our colleagues of the Planning Committee had in mind. More functionally, the idea seemed to be to make available whatever might prove useful to the members of this Association, in whatsoever capacity they might require.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE NONHUMAN ENVIRONMENT IN NORMAL DEVELOPMENT AND IN SCHIZOPHRENIA. By Harold F. Searles. New York: International Universities Press, 1960, xvii + 446 pp., $7.50.

The author describes various aspects, normal and pathological, of man's relationships to the external world of inorganic and nonhuman organic life. The importance of "man's kinship with the nonhuman environment" is detailed in a preliminary chapter. Following this, a chapter called "The Nonhuman Environment as Experienced by the Healthy Individual" takes up developmental phenomena in this area and describes "the psychological benefits which derive from a mature relatedness to one's nonhuman environment." The chapter devoted to psychopathology describes confusion between the self and the nonhuman environment, anxiety about being, or being considered to be nonhuman, "nonhumanness" as a defense against affective states, projection mechanisms, etc. Cultural attitudes are taken up in another chapter, and finally the author discusses the value of further investigations in his subject.

PSYCHOANALYTIC EDUCATION IN THE UNITED STATES. By Bertram D. Lewin and Helen Ross. New York: W. W. Norton & Co., 1960, xviii + 478 pp., $10.00.

The intention of the authors is, avowedly, "to present facts rather than preconceptions …" The "facts" cover the widest possible range, and are presented with wit and a wealth of literary allusion. They comprise such matters of general interest as historical considerations, concepts of psychoanalytic education and discussions of "the psychoanalytic student, " and specific studies of finances, methods and problems of selection, details concerning training analyses, the training analyst, supervision, teaching curriculum, child analysis, research projects, etc. It is impossible to discover a significant omission in this survey of psychoanalytic education in the United States.

THE UNCONSCIOUS BEFORE FREUD. By Lancelot Law Whyte. New York: Basic Books, 1960, xiii + 219, pp., $4.50.

Book Notices

THE LIFE AND WORK OF SIGMUND FREUD. By Ernest Jones. Edited and Abridged in One Volume by Lionel Trilling and Steven Marcus. With an Introduction by Lionel Trilling. New York: Basic Books, 1961. xxvi + 541 pp., $7.50.

The elegance of Lionel Trilling's writing is contained in an introduction tender in its tribute to the subject of this biography, now reduced in size to one volume by the emendation of most of the technical discussions of Freud's work. As Trilling says, "The work is large, and ordered, and courageous, and magnanimous in intention; and of the life we can say nothing less."

MAN'S PICTURE OF HIS WORLD. A PSYCHO-ANALYTIC STUDY. By Roger E. Money-Kyrle. New York: International Universities Press, 1961, 190 pp., $4.50.

The author here attempts to systematize psychoanalytic theory according to Melanie Klein, whose "school" he considers to be the "most progressive" one. Says Money-Kyrle: "To know all about a person's world-model, and how it was constructed, is … to know all about this person."

In Part I, the author deals with the construction of "world-models" in the course of development, approaching the problem from the biological, psychological, and subjectivist philosophical points of view, employing the well-known Kleinian formulations. In Part II, he attempts to apply these to demonstrate "how some of our evaluations in aesthetics, ethics and politics are determined by our world-models, and how it may be possible to distinguish evaluations determined by false models from evaluations determined by true ones." Such distinctions, to give a specific example, might comprise an inquiry as to "whether a man's belief that he was a deprived child has determined his ethics, whether the belief is true, or a myth invented to hide an unconscious sense of having been greedy and ungrateful; and whether, should he discover his belief to be untrue, his ethics will change with it, and in what direction."

PERSONALITY STRUCTURE AND HUMAN INTERACTION. THE DEVELOPING SYNTHESIS OF PSYCHODYNAMIC THEORY. By Harry Guntrip. New York: International Universities Press, 1961, 456 pp., $7.50.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's "Double": Arthur Schnitzler

Frederick Beharriell Ph.D.

Frederick Beharriell

Frederick Beharriell  

IT HAS ONLY recently been recognized that valuable new facts and insights are vouchsafed by a study of Freud's relationship with Arthur Schnitzler.1 That Schnitzler, the Viennese physician-poet and contemporary of Freud's, wrote strikingly "Freudian" plays and stories has, of course, long been common knowledge. For many years it was widely assumed that Schnitzler was merely one of the earliest and most perceptive of Freud's many literary disciples. In 1953, however, it was demonstrated that the relationship was more complicated, that in fact Freud and Schnitzler had not only read one another's early publications2 but had then regularly read one another's works as they appeared; that Schnitzler had eventually referred to Freud as his "double"; and that Freud, late in life, had called Schnitzler his "psychic twin, " and described him as a pioneer and independent master of depth psychology (1). It seemed curious that in spite of all this, the two "doubles, " living quite near one another in Vienna and following each other's careers with interest, did not meet until June, 1922, when Freud was sixty-six and Schnitzler sixty.3

Submitted July 1, 1961.

1This paper is intended as a supplement to "Freud and Schnitzler—(Doppelgänger)" by Herbert I. Kupper and Hilda S. Rollman-Branch (10).2Schnitzler, for example, as editor of the Internationale klinische Rundschau, discussed some of Freud's publications as early as 1887, and had read The Interpretation of Dreams in 1900, a few months after it appeared. Freud wrote in 1906 that he had been acquainted with Schnitzler's work "for many years" (6, p. 95).3At least Freud recalled no meetings. Schnitzler, in his diary, indicates they may have exchanged a few "casual" words, perhaps at meetings of the medical society (6, p. 104).722

[[RunningHead]]

Group I for Study of Curriculum and Didactic Teaching—A Sketch of their Meeting and their Work

Royden Astley M.D.

Royden Astley

Royden Astley  

The principal purpose of my presentation is to offer you an opportunity to see, as reflected in the work of Curriculum Conference I, something of the immense importance and usefulness of the Educational Program of our Association. In 1959, as the Survey drew toward a close, even those of us who had participated only by offering moral support, and by responding to the efforts of the Survey team to get at the "truth, " could take great pride in the realization that American psychoanalysts were the only group to have undertaken such a task of stock taking. Moreover, it became clear immediately that ways must be found for capitalizing on our new information; and you know, or have heard this morning, what has thus far been done in that connection.

As Dr. Lewin has said, a study of curriculum by active teachers from the institutes appeared to COPE to be a good place to begin to involve the several faculties. The Group with which I participated in this work included Robert Cohen from Washington, Samuel Guttman from the Philadelphia Association Institute, Aaron Karush from Columbia, Eugene Pumpian-Mindlin from Southern California, Sarah Tower from Baltimore, and myself from the Philadelphia Society and Institute.

I shall quote from some of the briefing material in order to present the scope of the problem:

We shall need to descry and to describe, as a base upon which a curricular structure can rest, the place of psychoanalysis as a science in itself that is related in a unique and dynamic fashion to a host of other sciences and disciplines. From one standpoint it may be viewed, for example, as a sister science to such others as anthropology and sociology, capable of a lively and useful interchange of data and ideas; but from another—and this is unique—psychoanalysis, with its roots in biology, alone can offer not only a coherent view of individual psychic development and functioning, but also source data upon the reflections of those that form the subject matter of other disciplines.

[[RunningHead]]

Survey of Psychoanalytic Literature on Overt Male Homosexuality

George Wiedeman M.D.

George Wiedeman

George Wiedeman  

SUMMARY

The psychoanalytic investigation of homosexuality was initiated by Freud in 1905 in the Three Essays. Until then homosexuality was mainly considered a constitutional variant. Freud described homosexuality as a deviation in the choice of the sexual object. After a stage of early strong mother fixation the homosexual leaves this attachment behind, identifies with his mother, and takes himself narcissistically as his love object. Strong castration fear and retention of the erotic significance of the anal zone were also recognized as important factors leading to inversion. In 1915 Freud stressed the predominance of archaic and primitive psychical mechanisms in homosexuality. Aside from consideration of the vicissitudes of the sexual drives in the origin of homosexuality, the role of the aggressive impulses in the genesis of inversion was discussed even prior to the formulation of the new dual instinct theory. Structural concepts and ego psychology have been applied more and more to homosexuality.

The problem of classification of homosexuality has been a difficult one. A division of homosexuality into different types according to manifest behavior was attempted several times but has not been generally accepted. Freud essentially came to the conclusion that homosexuality represents an inhibition and dissociation of the psychosexual development, one of the pathological outcomes of the oedipal period. The analytic literature does not disclose any single genetic or structural pattern that would apply to all or even a major part of cases of inversion. Overt homosexuality

405

may appear in a person whose ego functions are seriously impaired, viz., in a psychotic, but also in a person whose ego is relatively intact. But even in the latter cases the presence of "primitive psychical mechanisms, " viz., splitting and denial, can be observed.

Problems of identification in homosexuality were already discussed by Freud in the Three Essays. There are many references to disturbances in the process of identification due to a variety of factors. In this context, early stages of the ego and libidinal development are particularly relevant. Deficient neutralization of instinctual drives on the one hand, and libidinization of certain defense mechanisms on the other hand, play an important role in the genesis of homosexuality.

There are not too many reports on transference, course in therapy, and treatment results with homosexual patients. Anna Freud gives examples of several overt homosexuals she had in treatment and outlines certain essential interpretations which were necessary to effect a change from homosexuality to heterosexuality.

It seems that further study of homosexuality from the point of view of structural theory, the consideration of early phases of libidinal and ego development, and examination of transference problems will bring further progress in our understanding of homosexuality. Psychoanalytic research is moving in this direction.

[[RunningHead]]

Ego Differentiation and Schizophrenic Symptom Formation in Identical Twins

Theodore Lidz M.D. , Sarah Schafer , Stephen Fleck M.D. , Alice Cornelison M.S. and Dorothy Terry Ph.D.

Dorothy Terry

Dorothy Terry  

FOR THE PAST seven years we have been studying intensively the intrafamilial environment in which sixteen schizophrenic patients had been raised. The methodology and a number of findings of the study have been reported elsewhere (4), (5), (14), (15), (16), (17), (18). Among the patients was an identical twin whose co-twin became extremely disturbed immediately after the patient was hospitalized. The patient was treated in the Yale Psychiatric Institute and continued in analytically oriented psychotherapy after discharge, while his twin managed to remain at home receiving analytically oriented treatment from a therapist who kept detailed process notes of each session.1 The parents and an older brother were interviewed repeatedly, and the interaction among family members was observed closely. All five members of the family were given a battery of projective tests. The study of these twins, together with all of the other members of the family, and the intrafamilial environment they created, permitted a unique opportunity to examine the special problems of ego development confronting twins and the impact of the family dynamics in shaping the personalities and the pathology of these twins in particular.

Presented at the Midwinter meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December, 1959.

From the Department of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine, New Haven, Connecticut. This research is supported by grants from the National Institute of Mental Health and from the Social Research Foundation.

Submitted March 4, 1960

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dreams and Masturbation in an Adolescent Boy

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein  

SUMMARY

The dream material from the analysis of an adolescent boy is presented in connection with other clinical data to demonstrate the use of dreams in leading to a discussion of masturbation and masturbation fantasies. From the dreams, clues regarding the form of the masturbation and the content of the masturbation fantasies were obtained. This assisted in the analysis of the masturbation and the elucidation of important methods of control and discharge of tension in this boy. In turn, these were related to particular behavior patterns symptomatic of his disturbance. In addition, attention is drawn to the element of motion, specifically riding, in the manifest content of dreams as a reference to masturbation. It is suggested that such dreams are included in a group of typical dreams incompletely described by Freud.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Technical Aspects of the Analysis of a Child with a Severe Behavior Disorder

Selma Fraiberg

Selma Fraiberg

Selma Fraiberg  

SUMMARY

The analysis of this behavior disorder reveals in specific ways how the aggressive and destructive behavior was employed in the service of defense.

In tracing the motive for a destructive act we saw very early in the analysis how a fantasy in which the child was in extreme danger was translated into an action in which the content of the fantasy was reproduced and the ego spared itself anxiety through identification with the aggressor and through a shift from passive to active. (As in the first dream reported by Roger, the fear of castration by the faceless man was immediately translated into an action in which the child performed the symbolic destructive act upon objects in the room.) This defense served painful memories as well as fantasies. When primal-scene material emerged in the analysis, the memory of an act perceived as destructive, and as "fighting, " was translated into aggressive acting in which destruction of objects and fighting was re-enacted and that which was passively experienced through observing and overhearing was transformed into action. Ultimately, the great danger against which the ego defended itself was loss of identity. The extreme negativism and obstinacy that characterized another aspect of Roger's behavior disorder was seen as a fear of surrender to the object, the fear of becoming one with the object (2).

The connections between the masturbatory fantasies and impulsive acting provided insights both into certain forms of erratic conduct and the role of isolation of affects in the behavior disorder. Anna Freud (1) has brought our attention to a form of social maladjustment that can be traced back to the complete suppression of phallic masturbation and the consequent acting out of the masturbation fantasies: "… the masturbation-fantasy is deprived of all bodily outlet, the libidinal and aggressive energy attached to it is completely blocked and dammed up, and eventually is displaced with full force from the realm of the sex-life into the realm of ego-activities." In Roger's analysis it could be demonstrated

365

that he periodically abandoned phallic masturbation under pressure of castration anxiety and in the erratic and apparently unprovoked periods of acting that followed we could trace a masturbatory fantasy.

But Roger also illuminated for me the process that led to abandonment of masturbation at certain times. On many occasions there was no evidence of a conscious struggle against the urge to masturbate. (The child who fights the urge to masturbate reveals the struggle in defense and resistance in analysis. The argument between the urge and the prohibition is seen in moral ambivalence, self-punishment, self-imposed taboos. He reveals the struggle in transference by identifying the analyst with the repudiated temptations and often behaves toward the analysis as if it were a bad habit that must be broken.) Through Roger I came to understand that at certain times when masturbation was apparently given up he lost "the good feelings" in his penis. His own description of this, in a period of the analysis that I have not included in this report, went as follows: "But my penis doesn't feel good when I touch it. It doesn't feel not nice, either. Not good and not bad. Nothing." In other words, anesthesia took place, probably on a hysterical basis, and masturbation could no longer evoke phallic sensations. Masturbation was then given up, not as the result of a struggle, but because of the loss of feeling and the inability to have sensations in the penis. The fantasies which had lost their connections with masturbation were now acted out, following the pattern described by Anna Freud.

One further connection is suggested in the acting out of the masturbation fantasy. In the earlier association of the fantasy with the act of masturbation, the fantsy was "made real" by the accompanying phallic excitations. (Another child once said to me, "The stories are more realer when I'm playing with myself.") I have wondered, then, if one of the elements in the acting out of the masturbatory fantasy is the attempt to make the story real through enactment, to recover the lost excitement, and to animate the fantasy gone dead with the penis. And since the fantasy had once served to excite the penis, is acting out also a magical device to bring back the lost feelings in the penis?

The most important part of the analytic work was that which

366

dealt with the defenses against affects and it was here that analysis encountered its strongest resistance. The absence of anxiety and guilt which distorted the early picture of the behavior disorder was seen finally as the achievement of the most elaborate defenses against affect. By identifying with the aggressor and by switching from passive to active, the ego escaped both anxiety and self-reproach. The simultaneous acting out of libidinal and aggressive fantasies and the provocation of punishment spared the ego the necessity of experiencing guilt. The isolation of affect which proved to be the most formidable resistance of all, served many purposes in the mental economy. The barely disguised oedipal fantasies and castration fantasies were rendered less dangerous by depriving them of affect, making them "not real." Further, by maintaining guilt and anxiety in isolation, Roger was able to enjoy masturbation and its attendant fantasies. We saw that each time analysis re-established the connections between fantasies and affects the anxiety experienced by the child was nearly intolerable.

The analysis of the transference resistance in the last period of treatment shed further light on both the libidinal and ego aspects of the active-passive conflicts in which the behavior disorder had been rooted. Both the passive longings for the father and the defenses against these wishes were revived along with anal masturbation. The fight with mother over bowel training which had extended well into the fourth year was re-enacted in transference along with castration dread. The negativism which had been one of the primary defenses and one that had served resistance throughout the analysis was seen as a defense against the danger of passive surrender to the mother, the "fear of loss of personality" as Anna Freud put it.

Some Effects of Pregnancy and Childbirth on Men

Wilbur Jarvis M.D.

Wilbur Jarvis

Wilbur Jarvis  

SUMMARY

The effect of pregnancy and childbirth on the male is described as a universally occurring phenomenon, its result depending on the later psychic equilibrium in relation to the earlier infantile situation. Cases are presented as illustrations and suggestions made as to the further significance of the recognition of the role of childbirth in the psychic life of the father.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Study of Pirandello's "Six Characters in Search of an Author"

Charles Kligerman M.D.

Charles Kligerman

Charles Kligerman  

PIRANDELLO'S Six Characters in Search of an Author, pronounced a masterpiece almost from the moment it reached the public, is nevertheless a most baffling and intriguing play. It has been variously described as "cerebral, " "psychological, " a portrayal of man's isolation from himself and the times, a study of the conflict of life versus form, of illusion versus reality, etc. When a work of art appears so puzzling and paradoxical, yet compels our attention so forcefully, it is usually a cogent indication that some strong unconscious communication is going on between the artist and his public, and that in the unconscious area, the discrepancies achieve some unity. In the following discussion I shall attempt a reconstruction of this unconscious communication.

In general there are two approaches to the psychoanalytic exploration of drama: (1) The play can be considered independent of the author's personality, and the characters and their interaction studied as valid psychological entities in themselves. (2) The play is viewed as a projection of the artists's inner world, making possible a reconstruction of the writer's conflicts and personality. The first of these applies to the great objective writers like Shakespeare or Tolstoi who deal with human nature in all its protean manifestations. It would be difficult to know Shakespeare's personality, other than its incredible richness, from his plays.

Submitted January, 1962

731

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Critical Examination of Freud's Concept of Bound Vs. Free Cathexis

Robert Holt Ph.D.

Robert Holt

Robert Holt  

SUMMARY

1. Freud used binding (and its opposites, freedom or mobility of cathexis) in over a dozen different ways as his theory developed. The major usages are: (a) to describe the inhibition and delay of discharge, the diminution of peremptoriness; (b) to describe the close union of cathexis and mental presentation; (c) to describe the building of structures.

2. Any discussion of binding and mobility must distinguish two kinds of energy: that of the instinctual drives (of whatever degree of neutralization) or drive cathexis, and neutral energy (hypercathexis). One can make this distinction without taking a stand on the question whether neutral energy is wholly, partly, or not at all derived from drive energy.

3. Various usages of binding by Freud refer to these different energies: inhibition and delay, and the binding of cathexis to idea refer to drive energy; structure building refers to hypercathexis. Corresponding terminological innovations are proposed, to clarify such distinctions as that between the slippery freedom of drive cathexis and the useful mobility of hypercathexis.

4. Defense (including inhibition) and delay are now handled by means of structural concepts and the energic concept of neutralization. Hence binding should not be used in such senses.

5. Binding of drive cathexis to idea is a conceptual usage essential to explain (in part) the distinction between primary and secondary processes, and this meaning should be retained.

6. Binding of hypercathexes, as in the formation of a structure, is an essentially different idea than the binding of drive cathexis, so the term immobilization is proposed for it.

7. At the extreme primary-process end of a continuous series of thought processes, drive energy is both free and not neutralized; at the secondary-process pole, it is both bound and neutralized. Nevertheless, binding and neutralization are independent (if partly correlated) processes, the degree of binding being coordinated to formal properties of thought, the degree of neutralization to its content. Both binding and neutralization are continuous and quantitative concepts, not dichotomies.

8. The binding of cathexis to idea is brought about by hypercathexis. An unsolved theoretical problem is how to conceptualize the three different effects hypercathexis may have on a presentation: it may bind its drive cathexis to it; it may make it focally conscious (attention-cathectic use); or it may inhibit its discharge or access to consciousness (countercathectic use).

9. The relation of primary and secondary processes to consciousness is therefore not a simple one; it does not necessarily happen that the drive energy of a conscious thought is highly bound. There does, however, seem to be a partial relation, in that consciousness brings about the recording of experience in the conceptual organization of memories, from which it is recoverable at will.

10. In the secondary process, ideas have both drive cathexes and hypercathexes; the bound state of the former provides the stability, the mobility of the latter the flexibility of this type of thinking.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Curriculum

Heinz Kohut M.D.

Heinz Kohut

Heinz Kohut  

I am appreciative of the honor of having been asked to prepare an account of the Conferences on Curriculum in which I participated. To report to you the facts—and the spirit—of these discussions is, however, not an easy task.

The members of our conferences came from four institutes: from New York, Boston, The State University of New York, and Chicago; and we met twice, each time for a two-day week end. We had no initial agenda, except that each group, as if to introduce itself, had prepared an informal report of its own curriculum. Thus, the Chicago group consisting of Drs. Maxwell Gitelson, Louis Shapiro, and myself, presented the Chicago curriculum; the New York Institute group (Drs. Lillian Malcove, Martin Stein, and Nicholas Young) presented theirs; this was followed by the Boston curriculum described by Drs. Eleanor Pavenstedt, Eveoleen Rexford, and Arthur Valenstein; and finally came the Long Island group (Drs. Mark Kanzer, Sylvan Keiser, and Sidney Tarachow) who discussed the curriculum of The State University of New York.

We discussed and elucidated a number of specific problems; what we prized most, however, was the broadening effect of the interchange among individuals from different locations as an antidote to the dangers of unrecognized provincialism. Our ability to communicate fruitfully with each other was furthered by a number of favorable circumstances. First and foremost, there was great homogeneity among the participants in their basic outlook on the essentials of psychoanalysis. Second, the four institutes in which we had acquired our pedagogical experience are similar in one important respect: they are comparatively large, as regards both their faculty and student body. Third, we benefited greatly from the participation of Dr. Bertram D. Lewin and Miss Helen Ross whose broad knowledge of psychoanalytic education in the United States added welcome dimensions to almost every topic that we touched upon. And finally, we were fortunate indeed to have in our chairman, Dr. Maxwell Gitelson, an excellent discussion leader, whose skillful summaries and tactful guidance kept us from losing our bearings.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Existentialism

Joseph Coltrera M.D.

Joseph Coltrera

Joseph Coltrera  

Existentialism, in its chosen role as chorus to our age chants a sad litany: we are born into this world alone, live in loneliness, and go out of this world alone. Death is sure and the nothingness beyond it arouses dread. We have known original sin, and guilt is the condition of our existence. We are separated, each from the other, by the tradition and ways of our present culture and times. Existentialism tells us that these are the conditions of existence, and to be aware of existence is to know them. To do something about his lot man must come to life with a sense of "engagement." To the given moment of "engagement" man must present himself as an intensity and entirety. To the existentialist, this moment is the one of truth, only to be known by going in over the horns.

Existentialism is most simply and best defined by the statement: "Existence precedes essence" (153). In Western thought we distinguish the nature, or essence, of a thing from its existence. By essence we denote what

Jean-Paul Sartre: BEING AND NOTHINGNESS (1943), tr. H. E. Barnes. New York: Philosophical Library, 1956.

Helen Merrell Lynd: SHAME AND THE SEARCH FOR IDENTITY. New York: Harcourt, Brace, 1958.

Victor Frankel: FROM DEATH CAMP TO EXISTENTIALISM, tr. I. Lasch. Boston: Beacon Press, 1959.

Soren Kierkegaard: EITHER/OR, A FRAGMENT OF LIFE, tr. D. F. & M. Swenson. New York: Doubleday Anchor Books, 1959.

[[RunningHead]]

Depersonalization and Derealization

Charles Sarlin M.D.

Charles Sarlin

Charles Sarlin  

SUMMARY

A case of depersonalization has been described on the basis of which the following conclusions are offered for consideration.

1. The mechanism of depersonalization is an ego defense, of varying degree, in which the symptoms of estrangement from the self may be the ultimate manifestation.

2. Depersonalization is a defense against powerful id drives

and their associated affects which are viewed, either realistically or neurotically, as a threat to survival.

3. Depersonalization operates by a withdrawal of cathexis from the self-representation and results in the feelings of estrangement from the self.

4. Depersonalization may result when the conflict between the parents becomes internalized within the ego of the child; and in the struggle between simultaneous hostile identifications with both parents, the identity of the individual is lost.

5. Derealization differs from depersonalization in that cathexis is withdrawn from the object representations and results in feelings of estrangement from others.

6. Depersonalization and derealization, like narcissism and object love, all deal with various aspects of instinctual relations of self to objects, and involve varying degrees of maturation or regression of ego function and identity.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Scientific Method, and Philosophy

Robert Waelder Ph.D.

Robert Waelder

Robert Waelder  

A Symposium on this subject was held at New York University under the chairmanship of Sidney Hook, on March 28-29, 1958, and the proceedings are now available in book form. Most of the participants in the Symposium were philosophers, while a few came from psychology, sociology, and other disciplines. Psychoanalysis was represented by Heinz Hartmann, Lawrence Kubie, Abram Kardiner, and Jacob Arlow.

The Symposium was opened with a paper by Hartmann on "Psychoanalysis As a Scientific Theory" in which the subject was treated in general terms and special attention was given to metapsychology. Hartmann's paper deserves careful discussion by psychoanalysts, but most of the participants in the Symposium could neither know nor appreciate the nature of the experience upon which his remarks were based.

Kubie enumerated the difficulties which stand in the way of exact formulations in psychoanalysis; these range from exclusive dependence on auditory data to the questionable justification for inevitable quantitative pictures of mental life, or to the difficulty of measuring these alleged quantities. Against these liabilities Kubie placed the assets of the analytic situation and of the rule of free associations.

Arlow, whose contribution came later in the proceedings, had by that time discovered that familiarity with the elements of the psychoanalytic method could not be taken for granted; his approach was therefore simpler and more didactic.

PSYCHOANALYSIS, SCIENTIFIC METHOD, AND PHILOSOPHY. A Symposium edited by Sidney Hook. New York: New York University Press, 1959; xiv + 370 pp., $5.00.

Submitted December, 1960

[[RunningHead]]

A Case of Castration Anxiety Beginning at Eighteen Months

Lisbeth Sachs M.D.

Lisbeth Sachs

Lisbeth Sachs  

EVER SINCE Freud (3) wrote that the castration complex should be a term reserved for the occasion when the idea of (narcissistic) deprivation comes to be associated with the loss of the male organ, castration anxiety has been thought of as occurring at four or five years of age (2), between the third and the sixth years (1), (4). A case which closely follows the classical pattern of castration anxiety and clearly shows the phobic defenses against it, but in which the time of the onset is remarkably early, is reported here as evidence that castration anxiety may occur much earlier than commonly believed.

Case History

Cyril was the son of highly intelligent, professional parents. His father had undergone intense analytically oriented psychotherapy. His mother had been in psychotherapy with me prior to conception, during pregnancy, and after the birth of the child. Treatment continued during the emotional disturbance described below.

The father was rather strict and demanding of the child. The mother was warm and giving. She was inclined to pick him up at his least suggestion, but when frustrated by the child she had the tendency to lose her temper and to scold him with long tirades.

Assistant Professor in Psychiatry, State University of New York Downtown Medical College.

Submitted March 6, 1961

[[RunningHead]]

Reality and Actuality—An Address

Erik Erikson

Erik Erikson

Erik Erikson  

I

ONE OF THE STORIES of Freud's preanalytic years which assume a mythological quality in our training is the event at one of Charcot's evening receptions when the master during a bit of shop talk about hysteria in women "suddenly broke out with great animation: 'Mais, dans des cas pareils c'est toujours la chose génitale, toujours … toujours … toujours'; … I know, that for a moment I was almost paralysed with amazement and said to myself: 'Well, but if he knows that, why does he never say so?' But the impression was soon forgotten; brain anatomy … absorbed all my interest." (14).

Since then we have come full cycle. Freud's elucidation of " la chose génitale " has revolutionized psychology, and this Association has become the representative of that revolution in this country. Heirs of radical innovation, however, carry a double burden: they must do together what the founder did in lonely years and also strive to keep ahead of the habituations which result from success. They may well, at intervals, ask themselves what they have come to know and what they on occasion say with "much animation, " without pursuing it with the momentum of discovery.

One such item, I submit, is our knowledge of human strength. We have all heard psychoanalysts (including ourselves)

Delivered at the Midwinter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, 1961.

From Harvard University and Austin Riggs Center.

Submitted March 15, 1962

[[RunningHead]]

Prospect and Retrospect—An Interim Report by the President

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

THE DECISION to present to you at this time an interim report on the state of some of our affairs and my views on certain of the problems with which we are now confronted had a two-stage period of development.

The first of these took place a month or two ago, when I was asked, as President of The American Psychoanalytic Association, to give such an address at the Annual Meeting of the West Coast Psychoanalytic Societies held in Los Angeles in mid-October. Since this was somewhat in the nature of a celebration for having this high office held by one of them, and things being relatively calm out there at that moment, the stress of my presentation then was on how this fact symbolized the geographic growth of psychoanalysis, which had its parallels psychologically, scientifically, and administratively. Yet I was led even at that time to considerations concerning our present status and particularly to viewing some of our most pressing current problems.

This might have finished it, except for the circumstance that in the meantime another situation within our organizational midst kept pressing with growing intensity and with an increasing crescendo, as well as with certain attendant phenomena which soon made it apparent that more was necessary, and was indeed ripe for the saying.

Presented at a Plenary Session at the Midwinter Meeting of The American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 9, 1961.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unconscious Birth Fantasies in the Ninth Month of Treatment

Gilbert Rose M.D.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose  

SUMMARY

This survey suggests that unconscious transference fantasies may not uncommonly make unconscious use of the first nine months of treatment as a biologically determined temporal framework within which to express themselves. Four case examples out of twelve in a five-year period are cited. A fifth case illustrates the same use of the ninth month following completion of treatment. The period of the first nine months of treatment appears to lend itself readily as a hidden vehicle of unconscious pregnancy and birth symbolism of manifest dream content, symptoms, and behavior, much as a day residue may offer itself as the manifest content of a dream. The full latent content behind the pregnancy and birth symbolism thus emerging is then a matter for detailed individual analysis in depth. Since unconscious birth fantasies at the end of the ninth month of treatment make highly economic and condensed use of the well-known analytic phenomena of transference, the ubiquity of birth fantasies, and the tendency of the unconscious to play with numbers, it should perhaps not be surprising that they are found quite frequently, although, as far as I am aware, attention had not been drawn to them heretofore. Their recognition may often clarify material and be of technical importance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Altered Ego States Allied to Depersonalization

Julian Stamm M.D.

Julian Stamm

Julian Stamm  

SUMMARY

1. The state of depersonalization not only represents a regressive, defensive device, but, more specifically, a return, for longer or shorter intervals, to a primitive, oral, undifferentiated state in which one of the overpowering motives is to withdraw cathexis from external stimuli, to relax, and fall asleep.

2. In the material presented, this particular symptom is especially pronounced in persons with deep oral fixations and seems closely linked to Lewin's oral triad. Further study is required to ascertain whether the deep oral fixation is a universal feature in cases of depersonalization.

3. Splitting of the ego occurs universally in this condition. Further study is needed to determine its relationship to more

malignant conditions, such as schizophrenia and multiple personality.

4. What appears to be especially noteworthy in the cases cited is the development of depersonalizationlike phenomena in a hypnagogic state in which there appears to be a concomitant libidinal and ego regression. The latter variety of regression is partial and reversible and bears similarities to Kris's controlled regression in the service of the ego, as well as to Gill and Brenman's findings in hypnosis.

5. Impulses from all levels may be warded off by the symptom as a defensive device and may achieve autosymbolic expression in the symptom itself. In this context, case 2 provided the most striking features of oral derivatives.

6. In case 2, this state was at times accompanied by a feeling of pleasant relaxation, and anxiety was occasionally absent or of minor consequence. It should be added, however, that in all instances, anxiety was present on other occasions, especially when the ego was threatened with the fear of impending dissolution.

7. My material seemed to provide a more specific combination to explain the process of depersonalization, namely, the capacity to develop the hypnagogic state and subsequent partial ego regression in individuals with pronounced oral fixations.

responseSet.title: The Role of the Dream in the Analysis of a Latency Child
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Dream in the Analysis of a Latency Child

Marjorie Harley Ph.D.

Marjorie Harley

Marjorie Harley  

SUMMARY

In this paper I have tried to illustrate, from the analysis of a latency girl who was heavily burdened by the pressures of her inner urgencies, how the dream not only provided a "safety valve" for the discharge of her excessive excitations, but also a means through which to substitute reflective thought for impulsive action. When, especially in the early stages of treatment, her ego could maintain a sufficient margin of distance from her instinctual strivings to enable her to stand apart from herself, as it were, and through the dream medium to glimpse some understanding of what heretofore had been bewildering forces beyond her control, her relief was enormous. I have selected such fragments from her analysis as dealt with her acute castration complex, and have tried to show how, as her ego gained increasing relief from her instinctual urgencies, the dream material gradually faded from the analysis, to reappear with her renewed tensions at prepuberty.

This patient was one among those latency children whose dream material has played a dominant role in the analytic process, and who comprise the minority of those whom I have treated. All of these children experienced considerable pressure from their instinctual strivings, although not always to the same degree as did this little girl, and sometimes only in certain stages of their analyses, as impulses would force their way to the surface, either as the result of stimulation from analytic interpretation, or from experiences encountered outside the analysis. In general, however, it has seemed to me that their use of dream material has tended to coincide with these periods of increased pressure. I would add that within this context of ego-id intimacy, there obviously must be sufficient balance of energies for the ego to experience the processes of the id and yet to maintain that margin of distance which allows for the secondary-process functions of observation and reflection; otherwise the result will be either withdrawal and contraction, or too massive and immediate discharge through action.

Perhaps one explanation for the opposing views expressed in regard to the frequency with which children use dream interpretation in their analytic work lies in the differences in technical approaches. While ruminating over this question, I have wondered,

287

for example, whether Anna Freud's statement that "there is nothing easier to make the child grasp than dream interpretation" (2) may not be related to the fact that she recorded this observation in 1926, prior to her formulations of the ego's mechanisms of defense. It may be that our current emphasis on defense analysis as our preferred pathway to the id, and our careful avoidance of such id interpretations as may interfere with developing sublimations, result in a greater respect for the latency child's endeavors to meet the maturational demands for supremacy of the secondary process, and conversely help him to circumvent the primary-process threat of the dream. To put this in other words, our present-day technique involves a more gradual and a more selective uncovering of id contents, while in the early days of child analysis, the "preparatory period" was the prelude to a more direct exposure of the id. In one sense, then, the earlier methods of child analysis may have come closer, at times, to a seduction of the child's instinctual side and thus may well have led to a generally higher level of tension within the analytic situation.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theoretical and Clinical Aspects of Overt Female Homosexuality

Charles Socarides M.D.

Charles Socarides

Charles Socarides  

In opening the panel on overt female homosexuality, Herman M. Serota stated that this constituted the counterpart of the panel on overt male homosexuality (1959). Many of the general propositions then discussed would not be repeated here. However, all phases of the development of object relations from the state of undifferentiation to the most intricately sublimated ones so often characteristic of both apparent genitality and true genitality would be of relevance. It is obvious that the clinical presentation of an entity in which the regressive selection of an object by an adult for sexual gratification according to the narcissistic pattern brings into strong relief and reconsideration all developmental aspects of object and selection relations. For a variety of reasons, overt female homosexuality has been a relatively neglected clinical entity in contrast to that in the male. Analysts of long experience report having treated only a limited number of such patients. Female homosexual patients experience less superego anxiety and also less reality anxiety about their problem. Serota remarked on Freud's technical handling of his now famous patient in 1920 and the difficulties inherent in the management of the female homosexual who would willingly listen to a reconstruction of her dynamics, but was unable to endure her hostility to the therapist and fled transference feelings rather than relive them.

If one considers the problem from the adaptational viewpoint, the ego of the female homosexual is confronted with an exquisitely delicate attempt at resolving the multiple simultaneous problems posed to it by drives, structures, and functions, e.g., the need to reconcile drives toward gratification with limited representations of external reality, and to select objects with regard to past and present percepts (male and/or female characteristics), and toward whom both libidinal and aggressive expression may be directed in varying proportions. Instinct defusion is an ever-present danger.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Technique for Self-Analysis of Countertransference—Use of the Psychoanalyst's Visual Images in Response to Patient's Dreams

W. Ross M.D. and Frederic Kapp M.D.

Frederic Kapp

Frederic Kapp  

SUMMARY

An innovation in technique is presented for the self-analysis of unconscious components in the analyst's reactions to his patient and to his patinet's transference. The visual images of the analyst in response to his patient's descriptions of dreams are used by the analyst as the starting point for the uncovering of previously unconscious countertransference.

Pertinent literature on countertransference, self-analysis, and visual imagery is discussed with the explanation of the development of this method.

Four examples of the use of this technique in psychoanalysis are presented; in addition, one example is given from the supervision of psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy, to illustrate how a modification of this method might have prevented a therapeutic failure.

This technique is consistent with recent emphasis on the inevitability of some countertransference reactions, and even of their being useful when they are recognized, in contrast with earlier tendencies to consider them to be undesirable contaminants.

A suggestion is made for the validation of this technique and for its use in analytical supervision.

Although its use presupposes the skill and motivation of the analyst for the constant pursuit of insight, the method provides a distinct and relatively simple procedure for facilitating the self-analysis of the countertransference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations

Samuel Ritvo M.D.

Samuel Ritvo

Samuel Ritvo  

In his introduction, Leo Rangell suggested that the panel look at the vast field of object relations from the standpoint of clinical observations with their concomitant technical aspects and examine the role of object relations in psychoanalytic theory. Of the many broad issues in the area of object relations, Rangell selected two for closer consideration in his introduction: (a) the role of attachment in the wider field of object relations; and (b) the role of object relations in the wider general theory of human behavior in the psychoanalytic theory of neurosis.

Clinical data relevant to attachment behavior are abundant and can be found in manifestations of loneliness, separation, and loss of love. Rangell reviewed his own interest in the subject starting with observations which focused on the role of the perioral zone or the snout area in bringing about the desired state of attachment. Rangell considered the snout area, which is embryologically, anatomically, and physiologically suited for the function of attachment, important for adhering to the source of narcissistic supply, together with the hand, the skin, and the musculoskeletal postural systems. Later Rangell's interest turned from a consideration of the particular executive systems involved to a more detailed study of the function itself. He then conceived of the need for attachment as the psychological counterpart of the similar physical and biological need for stability and support. In Rangell's view, the quest for attachment progresses genetically from the infant's need for contact with the mother's body, to other people, and finally to wider units, such as family, groups, and community. True object relationships occupy an integral and central part within this spectrum of relatedness and attachment. This universal need for attachment can be traced across a spectrum from the normal to the psychopathologic, and from the benign to the most malignant forms.
