Narcissism

James Bing M.D. and Rudolph Marburg M.D.

Rudolph Marburg

Rudolph Marburg  

After Maxwell Gitelson introduced the panel, Ludwig Eidelberg presented a paper "On Narcissistic Mortification." He defined normal narcissistic mortification as the emotional experience of an individual who is suddenly overcome by internal or external aggression. Under normal conditions the individual learns to surrender his infantile omnipotence and to face the more or less frightening limitations of his powers. Under traumatic conditions the narcissistic mortification is repressed, which prevents the individual from disillusioning himself of his omnipotence. Another narcissistic mortification may be used to keep the original one unconscious.

After describing narcissistic mortification in metapsychological terms, Eidelberg introduced some working definitions: (1) the concept that libido and destrudo are always active and may or may not always have a passive or active aim; (2) the human being may sometimes have to play a completely passive role. Without the latter considerations it would be impossible to understand the concept of external narcissistic mortification. Libido and destrudo appear in three different forms: primary narcissism, secondary narcissism, and object libido (destrudo). He illustrated the study of narcissistic mortification by an example, pointing out that the act of eating satisfies the object libido as well as the narcissistic libido. If eating cannot take place in the presence of hunger, both drives are dammed up. If this is due to external aggression, narcissistic mortification ensues, thus adding humiliation and helplessness to hunger. This then leads to a feeling of revenge in order to undo what has happened by actively repeating what has been passively experienced.

An increase in instinctual tension beyond a certain level gives rise to pain and the elimination of this tension gives rise to a feeling of gratification.

responseSet.title: The Differentiation Between Concrete and Metaphorical Thinking in the Recovering Schizophrenic Patient
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Differentiation Between Concrete and Metaphorical Thinking in the Recovering Schizophrenic Patient

Harold Searles M.D.

Harold Searles

Harold Searles  

SUMMARY

The so-called "concrete" thinking of schizophrenic patients is undifferentiated thinking—thinking in which there is no differentiation between potentially concrete meanings and potentially figurative meanings. This "thought disorder" is not confined to some limited realm of experience comparable with the stratum of thinking in the normal adult, for in the deeply dedifferentiated patient there is no such well-defined stratum in his subjective experience. The disorder permeates his whole affective life, and his whole perceptual experience of himself and of the world around him.

Schizophrenia so severe as to involve a marked "thought disorder" of this kind is usually mentioned, in the psychiatric and psychoanalytic literature, in fatalistic terms, as if it were tantamount to incurability. With prolonged and intensive psychotherapy in a favorable milieu, however, it can be resolved.

As differentiation develops, in the affective, perceptual, and conceptual experience of the patient, between literal and concrete experience on the one hand and metaphorical (and other types of figurative or symbolic) experience on the other hand, three features of this disorder become clear: (1) It can exist only in proportion as there is fluidity of the ego boundaries, and be resolved only in proportion as these boundaries become firmly established in the course of therapy. (2) These boundaries can become established only by degrees as the patient becomes able to face the intense and conflictual emotions against which the schizophrenic illness system was designed to shield him. Thus we discover some evidence that the modes of thought characteristic of the healthy adult are founded upon the awareness of emotion. (3) We see, in the gradually recovering patient, that it is specifically the fluidity of ego boundaries which, by reason of the undifferentiated experience to which that fluidity gives rise, has been shielding the patient from the awareness of these emotions. Thus we find that the dissolution of ego boundaries is not merely a tragic end result of the schizophrenic process, but one of the formidably energetic defense mechanisms comprising that process.

The etiology of this "thought disorder, " both in terms of the

47

early relationship between the patient and his mother, and in terms of the family constellation as a whole, is discussed. The emotional impact of the advancing differentiation process, upon therapist as well as patient, is described. A few technical suggestions as to how one can assist the patient in achieving the differentiation between concrete and metaphorical are included.

responseSet.title: On Enthusiasm
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Enthusiasm

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

ENTHUSIASM IS A happy and joyous state of mind. It is a condition of high spirits—a special form of elation. Enthusiasm has some of the buoyancy of euphoria and the activity of mania, and it is obviously different from the blissful and peaceful elations. It is my aim to differentiate enthusiasm from other varieties of elation and to distinguish several types of enthusiasm. Then I shall attempt to formulate some of the metapsychological elements which make for the phenomenon of enthusiasm.

I

Enthusiasm is a passionate state of mind. It is exciting, active, and noisy—not quiet or passive like bliss. In this regard it resembles the hypomanias, only the activities are more realistic and adaptive. The more incongruous or bizarre the activities, the more likely that we are dealing with pathological enthusiasm or a hypomanic state.

There is an air of extravagance and expansiveness about enthusiasm—a readiness to use superlatives. The enthusiastic person does not merely feel good or even very good, but great—in fact, "the greatest!" There is a sense of exuberance, richness, an abundance of good fortune; yet with it all, there is some awareness that one is exaggerating; but it is enjoyable, and one is reluctant to give it up.

Presented as the Plenary Lecture at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in New York, December 11, 1960.

Submitted June, 1961

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Principle of "Working Through" in Psychoanalysis

Samuel Novey M.D.

Samuel Novey

Samuel Novey  

SUMMARY

Until now, the principle of working through has not been fully integrated into the body of psychoanalytic theory. The factors making for this deficiency have been discussed; special emphasis was placed on the role of affective experience and learning theory as additional clarifying principles. While it is recognized that the process of working through results in the acceptance of insightful interpretations, with their potential impact as reorganizing experiences, it is difficult to understand the time lag involved. It is suggested that the affectional, cue and reward aspects of the transference situation itself throw considerable light on the need for time in the working-through process.

Psychoanalysis sets as its goal the establishment of relative freedom from neurosis and characterological reorganization, which result in a psychic state substantially more satisfactory than that which existed when the patient came for treatment. In this sense it differs from the simply supportive psychiatric therapies, which generally aim at overcoming a temporary emotional decompensation and re-establishing the state of affairs existing before the acute episode, with all of the latter's potentialities for repetition of such episodes. The role of psychoanalysis in opening the way to new modes of functioning would imply that the establishment of normal characterological defensive operations would of necessity have to replace the pathological ones; these are not simply reinforced but are in fact done away with. If, as I have suggested, these two processes occur concomitantly, an otherwise inexplicable time lag incident to the process of working through would seem to be a necessary condition for achieving therapeutic results.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Features of the Dream Function of a Severely Disturbed Young Child

Erna Furman

Erna Furman

Erna Furman  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to outline three aspects of the relation between this boy's ego and his dreaming:

1. When his ego becomes strong enough to give up, in reality, a pathological behavior pattern which serves defensive purposes, the underlying conflict appears in intense dream activity. Many causative factors surely interact in this sequence of events, and the material allows speculation on some of them. The facet which impresses me most strongly is the following: when Danny's ego felt itself capable of dealing with the pressure of certain internal conflicts, or when the pressure itself became less intense, he could relax his defenses. In waking life this had the effect of his giving up defensive behavior patterns; in sleep it produced an increased dream activity. The latter may mean that the ego actually "allowed" more dreaming or that it now permitted him to remember his dreams.

2. When the reality becomes less threatening to Danny and he feels himself in a better position to cope with it, his ego allows certain of its own normal functions to appear first in dreams at a time when it still wards them off in waking life. In this instance, one can see the ego's attitude toward external pressures. Danny experienced his environment as traumatically stimulating, and his ego developed specific defenses to ward off the constant threat of being overwhelmed. Sleep, in contrast to waking life, protected him from the dangers of reality, and his ego could therefore relax

its defenses. The particular defenses that Danny's ego employed to ward off overstimulation had the effect of making him appear like a child in the undifferentiated state, overwhelmed with impulses and capable of primary process only. I wish to stress, however, that this picture was only the superficial one, presented to the observer, and resulting from the use of strong primitive defenses. The study of the patient's dreams revealed that his ego was capable of functioning on a much more advanced level than one would have assumed from his presenting defensive symptomatology. During the safety of sleep, his ego could afford to drop the distorting defenses so that more normal ego functions could be observed in his dreams.

3. When instinctual contents of dreams become too threatening to Danny's ego, it not only detaches from itself the content of the dream but even disowns the whole function of dreaming. This particular defense Danny appears to have derived from specific aspects of his early relationship with his mother.
